text:
Nitrogen centres acting as hydrogen bond or Brønsted acid acceptors in solid molecular acidbase complexes have been investigated using N 1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) in conjunction with $^{15}$N solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) spectroscopy together with X-ray diffraction (XRD) to correlate electronic and magnetic centres with their sites within the solid state. We show that large changes in binding energy following protonation together with the fact that salts and neutral co-crystals can give rise to very different XPS spectra signify that the changes in the N 1s binding energy of nitrogen centres in acidbase complexes can serve as a sensitive diagnostic technique for the assessment of the nature of nitrogen centres in protonated salts and in neutral co-crystals of acids and bases. This is substantiated by large low frequency ssNMR shifts occurring following protonation of the base by the acid whilst changes in the NMR spectrum of the co-crystal are normally small except for very strong hydrogen-bonded species.

By employing CASTEP-DFT modelling we show that density functional theory strongly correlates with experimental $^{15}$N ssNMR isotropic shifts, enabling modeling of the effects of the hydrogen-bonding interactions on the NMR parameters which can be used to differentiate between a salt versus a co-crystal form of a compound, demonstrating NMR crystallography. Our XPS results correlate very strongly with the results obtained from modelling and $^{15}$N ssNMR that demonstrates that the approach is valid and that the information provided by the technique(s) can be used to elucidate the local structure at the surface of materials or in non-crystalline regimes such as in amorphous systems. Overall, 17 systems were studied demonstrating wide scope of applications of multi-spectroscopic approach across organic, physical and materials chemistry.
